attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with vaginal bleeding and left-sided pelvic pain. She describes the pain as moderate, around 6/10 in intensity. She has no other medical or surgical history of significance. Vital signs are within normal range.

An ultrasound is performed, which confirms the presence of an ectopic pregnancy in the left fallopian tube. The adnexal mass measures 38mm on ultrasound and a fetal heart beat is reported. Her beta-hCG is 3000.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management in this case?
attributes.explanation:
Expectant management is incorrect. This woman is presenting with pain, a beta-hCG over 1000 and conclusive ultrasound findings of a live ectopic pregnancy. All of which are contraindications for expectant management of an ectopic pregnancy.

Dilation and curettage is incorrect. This woman does require surgical management of the ectopic pregnancy, however a dilatation and curettage is the surgical management of a non-viable intrauterine pregnancy.

Methotrexate is incorrect. This is the medical management of ectopic pregnancy. This is contraindicated in this woman as the pregnancy is >35mm in size, it has a heartbeat and her pelvic pain is moderate.

Laparoscopic salpingectomy is correct. Surgical management of the ectopic pregnancy is indicated and laparoscopic salpingectomy is the recommended procedure for a patient without contralateral uterine tube pathology. Salpingostomy could be performed if there was contralateral fallopian tube disease.

Misoprostol is incorrect. This would be the medical management for a woman presenting with a non-viable intrauterine pregnancy.
learningPoints: Laparoscopic salpingectomy is the recommended procedure for the surgical management of ectopic pregnancy where there is no contralateral uterine tube disease.
Medical management of ectopic pregnancy is contraindicated if there is a fetal heart rate on ultrasound.
Medical management of ectopic pregnancy is contraindicated if the adnexal mass is >35mm, there is significant pain or the woman is at risk of being lost to follow-up.
references: Ozcan MCH, Wilson JR, Frishman GN. A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Surgical Treatment of Ectopic Pregnancy with Salpingectomy versus Salpingostomy. J Minim Invasive Gynecol. 2021 Mar;28(3):656-667. doi: 10.1016/j.jmig.2020.10.014. Epub 2020 Oct 24. PMID: 33198948.

Po L, Thomas J, Mills K, Zakhari A, Tulandi T, Shuman M, Page A. Guideline No. 414: Management of Pregnancy of Unknown Location and Tubal and Nontubal Ectopic Pregnancies. J Obstet Gynaecol Can. 2021 May;43(5):614-630.e1. doi: 10.1016/j.jogc.2021.01.002. Epub 2021 Jan 13. PMID: 33453378.

SA Maternal, Neonatal & Gynaecology Community of Practice. Bleeding & Pain in Early Pregnancy: Ectopic Pregnancy, Miscarriage & PUL.  Department for Health and Wellbeing, Government of South Australia. 2019.

Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy: Green-top Guideline No. 21. BJOG. 2016 Dec;123(13):e15-e55. doi: 10.1111/1471-0528.14189. Epub 2016 Nov 3. Erratum in: BJOG. 2017 Dec;124(13):e314. PMID: 27813249.
